learning_objectives: Define quality in the project management context
Distinguish between quality planning, assurance, and control
Understand the cost of quality framework

lecture_notes.introduction: Quality means meeting requirements and fitness for use. It is planned in, not inspected in.
key_concepts: Quality definition: degree to which deliverables meet requirements
Grade vs. quality: low grade can be acceptable, low quality is never acceptable
Cost of quality: prevention costs, appraisal costs, internal failure, external failure
Quality management plan: standards, metrics, processes, tools
Continuous improvement: plan-do-check-act cycle
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating cost of quality for a manufacturing project comparing prevention vs. failure costs

review.value: Investing in prevention and appraisal reduces the much higher costs of internal and external failure
A low-grade product (fewer features) can be high quality (no defects); quality is non-negotiable

learning_objectives: Implement quality assurance activities including audits
Apply process improvement methodologies (PDCA, Six Sigma DMAIC)
Use quality audits to verify process compliance
readings: Pyzdek, The Six Sigma Handbook, Ch. 2-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Quality assurance focuses on processes. If the processes are right, the products will be right.
key_concepts: Quality assurance: systematic activities to ensure processes meet quality standards
Process audits: verifying that project processes are being followed
PDCA cycle: Plan-Do-Check-Act for continuous improvement
DMAIC: Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control for Six Sigma
Process capability: measuring whether a process can consistently produce within specifications
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a mock quality audit on a software development process using a checklist

review.value: Plan the improvement, Do the experiment, Check results against expectations, Act to standardize or adjust
Independent review to determine whether project activities comply with policies, processes, and procedures

learning_objectives: Apply the seven basic quality tools
Distinguish between common cause and special cause variation

lecture_notes.introduction: Statistical tools let us objectively measure whether a process is in control and capable of meeting specifications.
key_concepts: Seven basic quality tools: cause-and-effect diagram, flowchart, check sheet, Pareto chart, histogram, control chart, scatter diagram
Control charts: upper and lower control limits, centerline, plotting sample data
Common cause variation: inherent in the process, within control limits
Special cause variation: assignable causes, outside control limits
Rule of Seven: seven consecutive points on one side of the mean indicates a process shift
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a control chart from sample inspection data and identifying out-of-control points

review.value: Process is in control when points fall randomly within UCL and LCL; patterns signal problems
80% of defects come from 20% of causes; focus improvement on the vital few

learning_objectives: Design inspection and testing plans for project deliverables
Document and manage quality control results

lecture_notes.introduction: Quality control monitors specific project results to determine if they comply with relevant quality standards.
key_concepts: Inspection: examining deliverables to determine conformance to standards
Testing: executing a procedure to verify functionality or performance
Acceptance sampling: inspecting a sample rather than 100% of items
Defect repair: fixing non-conforming deliverables
Validated changes: verified that defect repairs were effective
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a test plan for a software module with entry/exit criteria and test cases

review.value: Prevention (building quality in) is cheaper than inspection (finding defects after the fact)
Statistical method for deciding whether to accept or reject a batch based on a sample; defined by AQL and LTPD

class_schedule.topic: Total Quality Management and Lean Principles
learning_objectives: Apply TQM principles to project environments
Identify and eliminate waste using Lean concepts
Integrate quality management with project governance
readings: Deming, Out of the Crisis, Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: TQM and Lean extend quality beyond inspection into a philosophy of continuous improvement and waste elimination.
key_concepts: TQM principles: customer focus, total participation, process-centered, integrated system, continuous improvement
Deming's 14 points for management
Lean wastes (TIMWOODS): transport, inventory, motion, waiting, overproduction, overprocessing, defects, skills underuse
Value stream mapping: identifying value-added vs. non-value-added activities
Kaizen: continuous small improvements driven by frontline workers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Value stream mapping exercise for a document approval process, identifying waste and proposing improvements

review.value: Organization-wide approach where every function and employee is responsible for quality
Any activity that consumes resources without adding value to the customer is waste to be eliminated

class_schedule.topic: Quality Metrics, Reporting, and Lessons Learned
learning_objectives: Define and track quality metrics throughout the project
Create quality dashboards for stakeholder reporting
Capture and apply quality lessons learned

lecture_notes.introduction: What gets measured gets managed. Quality metrics provide objective evidence of whether quality standards are being met.
key_concepts: Quality metrics: defect density, test coverage, customer satisfaction, process compliance rate
Dashboards: visual displays of key quality indicators for management
Trend analysis: tracking metrics over time to identify improvement or degradation
Quality lessons learned: what worked, what did not, what to do differently
Organizational process asset updates: feeding quality data back to the organization
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a quality dashboard in a spreadsheet with defect trends, test results, and compliance rates
setup_requirements: Sample quality data from a completed project

review.value: Leading metrics (test coverage, audit compliance) predict future quality; lagging metrics (defect counts) measure past quality
Capture throughout the project, not just at closing; store in organizational knowledge base for future projects

questions.question: A project spent $30,000 on training and process design (prevention), $20,000 on inspections and testing (appraisal), $15,000 on rework (internal failure), and $45,000 on warranty claims (external failure). Calculate the total cost of quality and explain what the data suggests about the project's quality approach.
questions.answer: Total cost of quality = $30,000 + $20,000 + $15,000 + $45,000 = $110,000. Conformance costs = $50,000 (prevention + appraisal). Non-conformance costs = $60,000 (internal + external failure). The data suggests insufficient investment in prevention, as external failure costs ($45,000) are the largest category. Increasing prevention spending would likely reduce the much more expensive failure costs.

questions.question: Explain the difference between common cause variation and special cause variation, and describe the appropriate management response to each.
questions.answer: Common cause variation is inherent in the process and produces random fluctuation within control limits. It can only be reduced by fundamentally changing the process (management responsibility). Special cause variation is due to assignable factors outside normal process behavior, shown as points beyond control limits or patterns. It should be investigated and eliminated by identifying and removing the specific cause (often operator or environmental). Reacting to common cause as if it were special cause (tampering) makes the process worse.

questions.question: Name three of Deming's 14 points that are most relevant to project management and explain why each matters.
questions.answer: 1) 'Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality' - in PM, this means building quality into processes and deliverables rather than relying on testing to catch defects. 2) 'Drive out fear, so that everyone may work effectively' - team members must feel safe reporting problems early rather than hiding issues until they become crises. 3) 'Institute training on the job' - project teams need proper training in methods and tools rather than being expected to figure things out through trial and error.

questions.question: Identify three types of waste (from the Lean TIMWOODS framework) that commonly occur in project management and provide a specific example of each.
questions.answer: 1) Waiting: team members idle because approvals are delayed or predecessors are not complete (e.g., developers waiting 5 days for design review sign-off). 2) Overprocessing: producing deliverables with more detail or polish than required (e.g., creating a 50-page requirements document when a 10-page version meets stakeholder needs). 3) Defects: deliverables that fail quality checks and require rework (e.g., code that fails testing and must be rewritten, doubling the development effort).

major_assignments.title: Quality Management Plan and Process Improvement Proposal
major_assignments.description: Develop a quality management plan for a project and propose a process improvement using Lean or Six Sigma methodology, supported by quality tools and metrics.
objectives: Create a complete quality management plan
Apply statistical quality tools to real or simulated data
Design a process improvement using PDCA or DMAIC
Define quality metrics and a monitoring dashboard
deliverables: Control chart analysis with at least 20 data points
Cause-and-effect diagram for a quality problem
Process improvement proposal using PDCA or DMAIC (3-4 pages)

criteria: Plan covers quality standards, metrics, tools, and responsibilities
Cost of quality analysis is included
Quality objectives are measurable and aligned with project objectives
Control chart correctly constructed with UCL, LCL, and centerline
Data interpretation identifies common vs. special cause variation
Cause-and-effect diagram is thorough and categorized
PDCA or DMAIC methodology correctly applied
Root cause analysis is thorough and evidence-based
Proposed improvements are specific, measurable, and feasible
Dashboard includes both leading and lagging metrics
Visual design is clear and actionable
Metrics are aligned with quality objectives

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on quality concepts, tools, and methodologies

quality_project.description: Quality management plan and process improvement assignment

midterm_examination.description: Quality planning, assurance, and statistical tools

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all quality management topics

class_participation.description: Participation in quality workshops, audit exercises, and case studies

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Quality workshop sessions require in-person participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: Workshop exercises are collaborative. The quality management plan assignment is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Data fabrication in statistical analyses is treated as academic dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Spreadsheet application with charting capability required. Statistical software (Minitab trial) recommended.
